---
title: "1.3 Expressions and Output"
subtitle: "Part 3: Remainder Operator and Pay Calculator"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## The Remainder Operator

### `%`

The operator `%` in Java is the <span class="term">remainder</span> (余数) operator.

Like the other arithmetic operators, it takes two operands.

Mathematically it returns the remainder after dividing the first number by the second, using truncating integer division.

## Remainder Example

### `5 % 2`

For instance, `5 % 2` evaluates to `1` since `2` goes into `5` two times with a remainder of `1`.

::: {.image-fit}
![Long division showing the integer result and the remainder](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-3-expressions-and-output/mod-py.png){fig-align="center" width="34%"}
:::

## Remainder and Mod

### AP exam boundary

Sometimes people call `%` the <span class="term">modulo</span>, or <span class="term">mod</span>, operator.

That is not actually correct, though the difference between remainder and modulo only matters when negative operands are involved and the signs of the operands differ.

With positive operands, remainder and mod give the same results.

`%` is all you need in the AP exam.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: lcop2`

Textbook prompt: In the example below, try to guess what it will print out and then run it to see if you are right.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: lcop2`

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(11 % 10);
        System.out.println(3 % 4);
        System.out.println(8 % 2);
        System.out.println(9 % 2);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: lcop2`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
1
3
0
1
```

## Remainder Pattern

### Smaller left operand

The result of `x % y` when `x` is smaller than `y` is always `x`.

The value `y` cannot go into `x` at all, since `x` is smaller than `y`, so the result is just `x`.

So if you see `2 % 3`, the result is `2`.

## Check Your Understanding

### `mchoice:: mcq-rem1`

What is the result of `158 % 10`?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `15`
- B. `16`
- C. `8`
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">C. `8`</span>

When you divide `158` by `10`, you get a remainder of `8`.

## Check Your Understanding

### `mchoice:: mcq-rem2`

What is the result of `3 % 8`?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `3`
- B. `2`
- C. `8`
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">A. `3`</span>

`8` goes into `3` no times, so the remainder is `3`.

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### Pay Calculator

In this coding challenge, you can work in pairs to create a pay calculator using math expressions and operators.

::: {.image-fit}
![Dollar sign](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-3-expressions-and-output/dollarSign.png){fig-align="center" width="14%"}
:::

Complete the following expressions for a pay calculator.

## Starter Code

### `activecode:: challenge1-3-pay-calculator`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Challenge1_3_Pay_Calculator
{
   public static void main(String[] args)
   {
       // Put in the math operator between 4 and 10 below to compute 
       // the pay for 4 hours of work at 10 dollars per hour.
       System.out.println("Pay for 4 hours of work at 10 dollars an hour");
       System.out.println(4  10);
       
       // Put in the math operator to compute the number of hours worked 
       // if the pay is 120 dollars and the rate is 15 dollars per hour.
       System.out.println("Number of hours worked for pay 120 dollars & rate 15 dollars per hour");
       System.out.println(120  15);
       
       // Put in the math expression to compute the pay 
       //  for 12 hours of work at 7.50 dollars per hour.
       System.out.println("Pay for 12 hours of work at 7.50 dollars an hour");
       System.out.println(        );
     
       // Add another statement to print the math expression to compute the integer number of 
       // hours worked if the pay is 100 dollars and the rate is 9 dollars per hour.
       System.out.println("Number of int hours worked for pay 100 dollars & rate 9 dollars per hour");
     


       // Add another statement to print the math expression to give the remainder when 
       //  100 dollars is divided by 9 dollars per hour. 
       System.out.println("The remainder of 100 dollars divided by 9 dollars per hour");
     

   }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: challenge1-3-pay-calculator`

Runestone checks that the output contains:

```text
90.0
```

for `12 * 7.50`.

Runestone also checks that the code contains these expressions:

::: {.tight-list}
- `4*10`
- `120/15`
- `100/9`
- `100%9`
:::

## Focused Work

### Required expressions

Fill the starter by using the right arithmetic operator for each prompt.

```java
4 * 10
120 / 15
12 * 7.50
100 / 9
100 % 9
```

Trace the difference between `100 / 9` and `100 % 9` before running.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain `%` as the remainder after integer division
- predict values such as `11 % 10`, `8 % 2`, and `9 % 2`
- use the pattern that `x % y` is `x` when positive `x` is smaller than `y`
- distinguish remainder from modulo at the AP exam boundary
- preserve starter-code prompts while completing the Pay Calculator expressions
- explain the difference between quotient `100 / 9` and remainder `100 % 9`
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
